title: Finding the Right Line
intro: A Python script is processing a list of student grades, but it crashes with: IndexError: list index out of range. It's like trying to grab the third book from a shelf that only has two -- you're reaching past what exists. The error points to this function. Can you find which line goes out of bounds?

teach.title: Line Numbers Are Like Page Numbers in a Book
teach.body: When someone says 'look at page 47, paragraph 3,' you can go straight there. Error messages work the same way. They give you a file name and line number so you can jump directly to the problem instead of reading through your entire codebase.

teach.title: Line Numbers Are Like Apartment Numbers
teach.body: If a friend says 'I live in the building on Main Street, apartment 4B,' you don't knock on every door to find them. You go straight to 4B. Error line numbers work the same way -- the file name is the building and the line number is the apartment. Go directly there instead of searching the whole codebase.

teach.title: How to Read the Line Number
teach.body: Error messages show the location like this: `File "app.py", line 12`. That means the crash happened on **line 12** of the file called **app.py**. Your code editor shows line numbers on the left side. Jump to that line -- the bug is right there or very close to it.

teach.title: An IndexError in Action
teach.code:
colors = ["red", "blue"]
print(colors[2])  # IndexError: list index out of range
teach.annotation: The list has 2 items at index 0 and 1. Asking for index 2 crashes because it doesn't exist. Python counts from 0, so a list with 2 items has indexes 0 and 1.

teach.body: Jumping straight to the right line number instead of guessing saves you hours every week. When you're building apps with AI to earn money, that saved time means more features shipped and more revenue generated.
Always look at the file name and line number in the error message first. It tells you exactly where to look. The bug is on that line or in the data being passed to it.

exercise.prompt: The error is IndexError: list index out of range. The list 'scores' only has 2 items but the function tries to access 3. Tap the buggy line.
exercise.explanation: When the list only has 2 items (index 0 and 1), accessing grades[2] crashes with IndexError because index 2 doesn't exist. The fix 'grades[:3]' uses Python's slice syntax, which safely returns up to 3 items without crashing if there are fewer. Slicing never throws an IndexError -- it just returns what's available. Option C (grades[0:2]) would only return 2 items, and option D uses JavaScript syntax that doesn't exist in Python.
